postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
i was wrong about indie startups. i can explain in 2 minutes.

i've been thinking a lot about arc browser lately. it's probably my favorite piece of software built recently.

but they just announced they're building a new ai-first browser. the original arc is basically on life support.

for the makers of arc, this is about scaling their business.

building a beautiful ui on top of chromium might get you a great product. but it won't get you to $100b.

i get it.

arc built something tech twitter fell in love with. including me. it's my daily driver.

but being well-loved isn't enough when you're vc-backed. you need to reinvent the category.

and that's okay. because here's what i've learned:

reinventing a category is a vc-backed startup game.

cash flow is an indie game.

never forget that.

it's a pattern i see everywhere:

notion trades simplicity for ai dreams and a mailbox

wordpress battles lawyers while ghost picks up

i call it the "mirror effect":

every time a vc startup is forced to reinvent, they create a $1-100m opportunity for indie founders.

the math is simple:

• vcs need billion-dollar exits  
• small markets don't work  
• original users get abandoned  
• someone needs to serve them  

smart indie founders don't compete with vc dreams.

they inherit the forgotten users.

the playbook is obvious:

1. build what made the original special.  
2. serve the users who hate "progress."  
3. stay "small" and profitable.  

you don't need to be a unicorn when you're making $10m arr with 80% margins.

sell that business for $50m-$100m. no investors.

there's a fortune in forgotten users.

and they're just waiting for someone to serve them.

i love arc. i hope it stays. but if it changes course, someone will continue that legacy.

maybe it should be you.

i used to think indie startups had to avoid competition with giants, but now i see they can win by embracing the forgotten markets.

some of the best opportunities aren't in the "next big thing."

they're in the markets vcs consider too small.

while everyone else chases billions

you can build a beautiful business serving the forgotten millions.

no right or wrong approach.

choose what excites you the most.

go big or go home.

or go small or go home.

each is beautiful in its own way.

what game do you want to pick?

author.name: Greg Isenberg
author.info: CEO of Late Checkout, a portfolio of AI native companies

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 year ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
At 20, you're dangerous because you don't know what's impossible. Every founder is building "AI for X" - you're asking why does Visa still hold your money for 3 days? Why doesn’t IG feel like a social app for friends anymore?

Your naivety is your secret weapon. Plus, you’re probably pretty broke so you have tons of ambition to make it.

Then reality teaches you expensive lessons. You launch a "worse" product but get distribution right. Your "MVP" gets traction but can't scale. Users love the product but won't pay. The feature everyone said they needed? No one uses it. 

Your “dumb” buddy from high school sold his company for $15M because he knew the director of product of Google. 

Your biggest competitor isn't other startups - it's inertia. Users hate their current solution but hate changing more. Customers enthusiastically saying "yes" in meetings never write checks. 

Your perfectly scalable tech stack doesn't matter when you can't find users. Your product never really answered the “why now” question.

By age 30, the game reveals itself. You see patterns others miss:

- Paper valuation of your startup is kinda BS
- The best companies start by solving a small, boring problem perfectly
- Consumer social is the hardest game in town (but fun)
- “X” is a bubble
- Failing marketplaces become SaaS companies
- Growing SaaS companies build marketplaces
- Every big company is a distribution company wearing product company clothes
- Your "clean" architecture matters less than your first 100 customers
- Most startup ideas are timing mistakes, not product mistakes

But you do start getting more jaded.

At thirty-five, you can't unsee these lessons:

- Profitability gives you ultimate leverage to do what you want in life
- Bad UI with distribution beats great UI without it
- Consumer indifference kills more startups than competition
- The best products look like toys to incumbents
- If you plan on selling your company one day, probably good to know the people in charge of making those decisions
- What you can't measure is usually what matters
- Market timing beats execution every time
- Edge cases reveal your true moat
- The best products solve problems so fundamental, they look obvious in retrospect

The words cash-flow sounds better and better to you.

If you're lucky, you learn the 35-year-old lessons at 21.

content:
consumer mobile apps are quietly back thanks to tiktok creators and AI 

if i wanted to make $1M with mobile apps right now, here’s the exact playbook i’d use:

1. find hidden opportunities on sensor tower.

search for apps making $30k+ monthly with basic or outdated AI, or non-AI apps that could use an AI edge. 

pick ones with clunky UI or weak UX—those are your goldmines. improve the look, add useful AI, and you’re on your way.

2. master one distribution channel. 

pick your niche’s top platform and lock into one format that works. post daily, and use claude to analyze the best posts in that space. figure out the language that drives engagement.

3. scale distribution through tiktok creators.

search tiktok shop hashtags in your niche + "link in bio" 

find sellers doing $10k+ monthly in physical products look for 40k+ followers and high engagement rates bonus if they're already promoting other apps

the big insight here is way easier to sell $4.99 app to a stranger than a $49 pillow to a stranger.

DM strategy that works: "saw you crushing it with [product]. we're building an app that solves [same problem] at $4.99. most users stick around 6+ months. 50% rev share on every sale you drive. interested in early access?"

start everyone on 50% rev share track performance for 90 days convert your top 3 to equity milestone-based vesting tied to revenue

4. launch quick, effective landing pages.

v0 for mockups, get inspiration from top app store screenshots, and set up a waitlist + a simple validation survey. throw ~$50 into tiktok ads to see if your copy converts before committing resources.

5. keep the build stack lean

 v0 for UI, claude for code, openai for AI features, and revenue cat for payments. minimize overhead so you can iterate fast and keep costs low.

6. scale and rinse

launch a related app and cross-promote with the first one. bundle pricing to lock users in and reuse your creator network to scale even faster.

congratulations, you didn’t just build an app—you built a mobile holding company.

seeing this playbook work again and again

last month saw a holdco

2 founders 
3 apps
0 VCs
15 creators on the cap table
72% margins
$120k/month in combined revenue
not based in silicon valley

it fires me up.

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 year ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
Most founders panic when their product flops. They slash prices. Run desperate promos. Eventually shut down.

But there's another way.

When a product fails in its intended market, you have two options:

1. Die slowly trying to force it
2. Embrace the rejection and find a new narrative

Look at what Crocs did:

• Started as boat shoes
• Rejected by boaters
• Embraced by chefs, nurses, kids
• Now they're "ugly-cool" fashion

Or Cognac:

• Failed as French wine competitor
• Embraced by hip-hop culture
• Became luxury status symbol
• Now it's a $3B+ market

If I'm Apple, here's the play:

• Partner with Jony Ive for limited edition
• Get Murakami to design custom frames
• Raise price to $5,000
• Make it art, not tech

Because what's more valuable:

A failed mass market product, or a collector's item that makes people fall more in love with your brand?

Sometimes your "failure" is just your product finding its true purpose.

Not as the next iPhone.

But as the frame in collectors' homes that drives word of mouth and high margins.

Gets you thinking, doesn't it?

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
i found probably one the greatest pieces of copy ever written. this marketing masterclass from cars can be applied to software, ai, marketplaces, agencies etc

the car industry is obsessed with proving value through features, specs, and promises, they chose to say almost nothing.

but in saying almost nothing, they said everything.

they understood something deeper about confidence in marketing

the less you say, the more weight each word carries.

leaves it to the imagination

perfect example of when you genuinely have something great you don't need to explain it you just need to hint at it

every extra word actually reduces the impact 
every added claim weakens the core promise 
every feature list kills the mystery

how to write clean copy that people love:

1. strip away everything that isn't absolutely essential the best lines have nothing left to remove
2. say the obvious truth you've been avoiding dancing around it makes you sound weak
3. write like you're telling a friend then delete half of those words
4. shorter sentences hit harder your copy isn't a novel
5. confidence lives in white space give your words room to breathe
6. stop trying to sound smart big words make small points
7. if it sounds like marketing delete it and start over
8. write drunk, edit sober then edit sober again
9. kill every adjective let nouns do the work
10. read it out loud your ear knows when you're lying

now, i drive a volvo, not a land rover, ive actually heard that land lovers arent that reliable, maybe people return every few months for maintenance lol... but hope this copy chat got your creative juices flowing.
